Name: infotheoreticquantizationbounds
Title: Information-Theoretic Bounds for Adaptive Mixed-Precision Quantization in Deep Learning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by deriving information-theoretic bounds on the distortion-rate trade-off for adaptive mixed-precision quantization, we can achieve a more efficient and accurate quantization of deep neural networks. This approach will provide a principled way to allocate bits to different layers, ensuring that the information loss is minimized while maintaining computational efficiency.
Related Work: Recent works such as HAWQ-V2 and GPTQ-intrinsic LoRA have explored mixed-precision quantization using second-order information and low-rank corrections, respectively. However, these methods lack a rigorous information-theoretic foundation that can guide the allocation of bits across layers. Our work builds on these efforts by providing a theoretical framework that quantifies the trade-off between quantization precision and model performance, ensuring that the allocation of bits is optimal and can be generalized across different architectures and tasks.
Abstract: Mixed-precision quantization (MPQ) is a crucial technique for reducing the memory and computational requirements of deep neural networks while maintaining performance. However, existing methods often rely on heuristics or empirical observations to determine the optimal bit-widths for different layers, lacking a principled theoretical foundation. In this work, we derive information-theoretic bounds on the distortion-rate trade-off for adaptive mixed-precision quantization. By framing the quantization problem as a rate-distortion optimization, we provide a principled way to allocate bits to different layers, ensuring that the information loss is minimized while maintaining computational efficiency. We validate our approach through extensive experiments on a variety of neural network architectures and tasks, demonstrating significant improvements in both accuracy and efficiency compared to existing methods. Our theoretical framework not only provides a new perspective on MPQ but also offers a robust and generalizable solution for practical applications.
Experiments: Derive the rate-distortion function for a deep neural network layer, considering the mutual information between the input and output of the layer as the distortion measure.
Develop an algorithm to compute the optimal bit-widths for each layer by solving the rate-distortion optimization problem using dynamic programming.

Implement the proposed quantization scheme on a variety of neural network architectures, including ResNet, MobileNet, and Vision Transformers, and compare the performance with existing methods such as HAWQ-V2 and GPTQ-intrinsic LoRA.
Evaluate the model's accuracy and computational efficiency on standard benchmarks such as ImageNet and COCO.
Analyze the sensitivity of the quantization scheme to different hyperparameters and provide a theoretical justification for the observed behavior.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The derivation of the rate-distortion function may be complex and computationally intensive, requiring efficient approximations or simplifications.
The proposed method may not generalize well to all types of neural network architectures, especially those with highly non-linear or specialized layers.
The optimal bit-widths may vary significantly across different tasks and datasets, requiring careful tuning and validation.
The practical implementation of the method may face hardware constraints, particularly in edge devices with limited computational resources.
